title: Quantum Implicit-Explicit Schemes for Multiscale Ordinary and Partial Differential Equations via Schrödingerization
authors: Qitong Hu, Xiaoyang He, Shi Jin, Xiao-Dong Zhang
abstract: In this paper, we present a quantum implicit-explicit (IMEX) scheme for multiscale ordinary and partial differential equations whose discretization parameters are independent of the scaling parameter $\varepsilon$. A key ingredient of our approach is a continuous-time formulation of classical IMEX schemes, which decouples the evolution time of the quantum algorithm from the physical time of the differential equation and is therefore particularly useful in multiscale settings. Building on this idea, we employ the Schrödingerization framework [Phys. Rev. Lett. 133 (2024), 230602] to implement IMEX schemes on quantum computers. Compared to previous HHL type quantum AP scheme [J. Comput. Phys. 471 (2022), 111641], this new method requires narrower -- an extra logarithmic factor -- auxiliary register numerical examples on linear heat and multiscale telegraph equations demonstrate the independence in $\varepsilon$ of the method.
summary: This paper introduces a novel quantum Implicit-Explicit (IMEX) scheme utilizing the Schrödingerization framework to solve multiscale PDEs on quantum computers. Its key breakthrough is achieving a query complexity that is independent of the small scaling parameter $\varepsilon$, a major hurdle in simulating stiff, multiscale physical systems. This advances the practical application of quantum computation to complex physics problems.
main_problem: Developing quantum algorithms to solve multiscale Ordinary and Partial Differential Equations (ODEs/PDEs) whose discretization parameters are independent of the small scaling parameter $\varepsilon$.
main_result: The proposed quantum IMEX scheme achieves query complexity independent of the scaling parameter $\varepsilon$, significantly improving upon previous quantum methods.
method: The approach uses a quantum Implicit-Explicit (IMEX) scheme combined with the Schrödingerization framework to map the differential equation into a unitary evolution suitable for quantum simulation.
model_or_system: The methods are applied to multiscale ODEs and PDEs, exemplified by the linear heat equation and the multiscale telegraph equation, which exhibit widely separated temporal or spatial scales.
key_observables: Query complexity, auxiliary register width, and global error bounds.
important_parameters: Scaling parameter $\varepsilon$, time step $\tau$, spatial step $h$, and the number of time steps $N_t$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the entries of the system matrices are of order $O(1)$ and that the solution remains bounded.
figures_summary: Figures show numerical results comparing the quantum IMEX scheme against classical methods for the 1D and 2D Heat Equations, demonstrating convergence across different $\varepsilon$ values.
paper_structure: The paper builds from general IMEX theory to the Schrödingerization framework, analyzes error propagation and convergence bounds, and concludes with specific numerical applications to the Heat and Telegraph equations.
why_interesting: The work provides a concrete quantum computational pathway for simulating complex physical systems (like those governed by multiscale PDEs) where traditional numerical methods struggle with stiffness, directly impacting quantum simulation feasibility.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Numerical results for Eq. (5.1) in the 1D case after Schrödingerization, shown at t = 0.1, with a(t) = 100/(t + 1) and u0(x) = 1 for x ∈[0, 1]. The spatial and temporal discretization parameters are ∆x = 2−4 and ∆t = ∆x2/2, respectively. Results from our scheme are shown by circular markers and compared with the classical-scheme solution shown by the black curve.

Fig. 2: Numerical results for Eq. (5.1) in the 2D case after Schrödingerization, shown at t = 0.1. Panel (a) shows the classical scheme, and panel (b) shows the quantum IMEX scheme. The spatial and temporal discretization parameters are ∆x1 = ∆x2 = 2−3 and ∆t = ∆x2 1/2 = ∆x2 2/2, respectively.

Fig. 3: Numerical results for Eq. (5.2) with a(t) = 0.5t+0.25 and β = 2 after Schrödingerization, shown at t = 0.1. The MATLAB implementation uses 16 interior spatial unknowns, ∆t = 0.5 ∆x3/2, and a support-compressed realization of the χ-based homogeneous extension with K = √Nx.
